turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion of the Court in case 16-341, TC Heartland versus Krafts Brand -- Group Brands.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari, the United States Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit. It requires us to determine the proper judicial venue for patent infringement lawsuits brought against domestic corporations. There are two venue statutes relevant to this issue, the Patent Venue Statute, 28 U.S.C. 1400(b) and the General Venue Statute, 28 U.S.C 1391(c). The Patent Venue Statute provides that any civil action for patent infringement may be brought in the judicial district where the defendant resides. In Fourco Glass Company versus Transmirra Products Corporation, this Court held that a domestic corporation resides only in its state of incorporation for purposes of patent infringement litigation. In reaching that conclusion, the Court rejected the argument that the Patent Venue Statute incorporates the broader definition of corporate residence contained in the General Venue Statute. Congress has not amended the Patent Venue Statute since this Court construded in Fourco, but it has amended the General Venue Statute twice. The General Venue Statute now provides that -- and I quote, “in part, for all venue purposes, except as otherwise provided by law; a corporate defendant shall be deemed to reside in any judicial district in which it is subject to the Court's personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question.” The issue in this case is whether that definition supplants the definition of resides announced in Fourco. When Congress intends to change the meaning of one law by amending a different law, it ordinarily provides a relatively clear indication of its intent in the text of the amended law. The current version of the General Venue Statute does not contain any indication that Congress intended to alter the meaning of the Patent Venue Statute as interpreted in Fourco. Although, the General Venue Statute now applies for all venue purposes, the version at issue in Fourco similarly apply for venue purposes. The addition of the word “all” to the already comprehensive provision does not suggest that Congress intended for us to reconsider Fourco's conclusion and in at least one respect, the current version of the General Venue Statute places our decision in Fourco on even stronger footing than when it was decided. The current version includes a savings clause expressly stating that the default definition does not apply when otherwise provided by law. Other provisions like the Patent Venue Statute -- here, thus may define resides differently that the default definition. Accordingly, we conclude that the amendments to the General Venue Statute did not modify the meaning of the Patent Venue Statute as interpreted in Fourco. We reaffirm that a domestic corporation resides only in its State of incorporation for purposes of the Patent Venue Statute. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Federal Circuit. The decision of the Court is unanimous. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
